patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 38-year-old woman, vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I’ve been exhausted for months and can’t get my energy back.” Volunteer only: extreme tiredness/weakness despite sleep, lightheaded on standing/near-fainting, always cold, low appetite with some unintentional weight loss, dry skin, and that you have diffuse systemic sclerosis with tight skin for years. If asked directly, add: periods never really returned after last pregnancy, couldn’t breastfeed/no milk, low sex drive, constipation, intermittent headaches since delivery, brain fog/slow thinking, nausea when stressed or skipping meals. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about delivery complications or hormones: severe postpartum bleeding needing urgent treatment, loss of underarm/pubic hair, puffy face/hoarse voice, decreased sweating, and episodes of shaky/sweaty/confused when you go too long without eating. Resist discussing vaginal dryness/pain with sex and feeling depressed/tearful; only admit if the clinician is very gentle and normalizes it. Misattribute symptoms to scleroderma, anemia, thyroid, or diabetes (polyuria/polydipsia). Express money worries: want the cheapest “one hormone,” doubt labs are necessary, and prefer fewer visits to avoid lost wages.

medications: Testosterone gel 1% 5 mg transdermal daily
Mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg PO twice daily
Nifedipine ER 30 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
confounders: Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis with fatigue, weight change, and cold intolerance overlapping with hypothyroid symptoms
Mycophenolate-associated cytopenias and chronic inflammation contributing to anemia and fatigue
Raynaud-related bradycardia/low perfusion symptoms that can mimic endocrine-related lethargy
Testosterone therapy causing acne/hirsutism and menstrual irregularity, potentially obscuring hypogonadism assessment
Possible low oral intake/dehydration from esophageal dysmotility/GERD contributing to hypotension and hyponatremia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hypopituitarism with secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism)
acceptable_alternatives: Central (secondary) adrenal insufficiency with central hypothyroidism
required_evidence: Low 8 AM serum cortisol with low or inappropriately normal ACTH (central adrenal insufficiency pattern)
Low free T4 with low or inappropriately normal TSH (central hypothyroidism pattern)
Low prolactin (supports hypopituitarism)
Compatible clinical history (e.g., postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension with subsequent failure to lactate, amenorrhea, fatigue, hypotension)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low (hyponatremia) due to cortisol deficiency/possible impaired free water clearance
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal (typically normal in secondary adrenal insufficiency; hyperkalemia suggests primary adrenal failure instead)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): Low or low-normal (risk of fasting hypoglycemia with cortisol deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (below reference range; may be very low in significant deficiency)
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated despite low cortisol)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated despite low free T4)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 10 mg AM, 5 mg early afternoon; adjust to symptoms and weight)
Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV once then 50 mg IV every 6 hours (or 200 mg/24h IV infusion) if adrenal crisis suspected, with transition to oral when stable
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start after glucocorticoid replacement is initiated; lower starting dose e.g., 25-50 mcg daily if older/cardiac disease)

Testosterone: hold until endocrine evaluation confirms indication; if hypogonadism confirmed and patient is male, use testosterone gel 1% 50 mg topically daily (or equivalent formulation) with monitoring; if patient is female, avoid routine testosterone replacement unless specialist-directed
non_pharmacological: Medical alert identification for adrenal insufficiency
Sick-day rules: double/triple oral hydrocortisone during febrile illness; use IM/IV stress-dose steroids for vomiting/major stress; seek urgent care for inability to keep fluids down
Patient education on adrenal crisis symptoms (severe weakness, vomiting, abdominal pain, hypotension, confusion) and emergency steroid use
Evaluate full pituitary function and etiology: pituitary MRI, assessment of gonadotropins/sex steroids, IGF-1; address fertility/menstrual issues as appropriate
Assess and treat precipitating factors; ensure adequate hydration and correction of hypoglycemia/hyponatremia as needed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Endocrinology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after initiating therapy (earlier: 48-72 hours if unstable or if adrenal crisis treated), then every 6-12 weeks for dose titration and monitoring

must_include: Condition is due to pituitary hormone deficiency (often after significant postpartum blood loss/low blood pressure) leading to low cortisol and low thyroid hormone
Low cortisol can be life-threatening (risk of adrenal crisis), and symptoms can include fatigue, dizziness/low blood pressure, low sodium, and low blood sugar
Thyroid labs reflect central hypothyroidism (TSH may not be elevated despite low free T4)
Replace cortisol first (hydrocortisone) before starting thyroid hormone to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
How to take hydrocortisone (split dosing, do not stop abruptly)
Stress-dose/sick-day instructions and when to use emergency steroid injection or seek urgent care
Levothyroxine dosing and monitoring will be guided by free T4 (not TSH) in central hypothyroidism
Testosterone should not be continued/initiated without confirming deficiency and appropriateness; discuss risks and monitoring if indicated
Discuss warning signs requiring emergency care (vomiting, severe weakness, fainting, confusion, severe abdominal pain)
Review current medications and clarify any steroid/thyroid side effects and expected timeline for symptom improvement
Confirm and document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and reassure that planned endocrine medications are unrelated
Carry steroid emergency card/medical alert ID and keep extra hydrocortisone available
Maintain hydration; avoid prolonged fasting if prone to hypoglycemia until cortisol replacement optimized
Adherence importance and need for regular follow-up labs (electrolytes, glucose, free T4) and clinical monitoring

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected hypopituitarism/Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension, lactation failure, amenorrhea, fatigue, dizziness, hyponatremia/hypoglycemia symptoms) and assess for adrenal crisis symptoms.
Order initial endocrine and safety labs to confirm central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism and assess complications (electrolytes, glucose).
Review labs to support diagnosis (low morning cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH; low free T4 with non-elevated TSH; possible hyponatremia/hypoglycemia) and guide urgency.
Make/communicate diagnosis of Sheehan syndrome with hypopituitarism causing secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm and document penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash) and ensure planned meds are safe.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; screen for interactions (e.g., levothyroxine absorption issues, anticoagulants, antiepileptics, iron/calcium) and steroid interactions.
Initiate glucocorticoid replacement first (hydrocortisone) and only then start levothyroxine if stable; avoid precipitating adrenal crisis.
Explain diagnosis, life-threatening risk of low cortisol, medication sequencing, sick-day rules, emergency injection/urgent care triggers, and monitoring approach (free T4-guided).
Arrange close follow-up for symptom check and lab monitoring (electrolytes, glucose, free T4) and longer-term pituitary evaluation/management.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, understanding, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Standard evaluation and management confirming Sheehan syndrome with secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism; initiates hydrocortisone before levothyroxine; provides sick-day rules and follow-up.
must_collect: history of postpartum hemorrhage or hypotension
dizziness/orthostasis or low blood pressure symptoms
symptoms of hyponatremia/hypoglycemia (nausea, confusion, sweating, tremor)
current medication list including any thyroid meds/steroids/OTC supplements
steps: ASK: postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension, lactation failure, menses changes, fatigue, dizziness, hypoglycemia symptoms, red flags for adrenal crisis, meds/supplements, allergies
ORDER_LAB: AM cortisol, ACTH, free T4, TSH, BMP (Na/K), glucose (± CBC), consider prolactin/LH/FSH/estradiol or testosterone as appropriate
GET_RESULTS: interpret as secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism; note hyponatremia/hypoglycemia if present
DIAGNOSE: Sheehan syndrome with hypopituitarism causing secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin urticarial rash; document
CHECK_INTERACTION: screen for levothyroxine absorption interactions (iron/calcium/PPIs), anticoagulants, anticonvulsants; steroid interactions as relevant
PRESCRIBE: hydrocortisone replacement first with clear dosing plan; then initiate levothyroxine once steroid started/clinically stable; avoid testosterone unless confirmed deficiency/indication

EDUCATE: explain diagnosis/pathophysiology, adrenal crisis risk, steroid sick-day rules and emergency injection/urgent care triggers, levothyroxine monitoring by free T4, medical alert ID, hydration and avoiding prolonged fasting, adherence and lab follow-up
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: early follow-up (days–weeks) for symptoms, BP, electrolytes/glucose; repeat free T4 to titrate levothyroxine; endocrine referral/long-term pituitary workup

minimal_paths.description: If symptoms suggest impending adrenal crisis, prioritizes immediate steroid treatment and emergency escalation while still meeting communication and safety requirements.
must_collect: vomiting or inability to keep fluids down
current meds and last steroid exposure
steps: ASK: crisis red flags, triggers, postpartum history, meds, allergies
ORDER_LAB: AM cortisol/ACTH if feasible, BMP and glucose urgently (do not delay treatment if high suspicion)
DIAGNOSE: suspected Sheehan syndrome with secondary adrenal insufficiency; high risk adrenal crisis
PRESCRIBE: immediate glucocorticoid replacement (and instruct urgent ED care if unstable or unable to take PO); defer levothyroxine until steroids started/stable
EDUCATE: emergency warning signs, sick-day rules, emergency injection/urgent care, medical alert ID
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: expedited endocrinology and close reassessment; later add levothyroxine with free T4 monitoring

acceptable_variants.description: Includes broader pituitary evaluation and imaging planning while maintaining correct treatment sequence.
steps: ORDER_LAB (includes prolactin, LH/FSH, estradiol/testosterone as appropriate, IGF-1; consider pregnancy test when relevant; lipid panel/hematocrit if considering testosterone)
PRESCRIBE (hydrocortisone first, then levothyroxine; no testosterone unless confirmed/indicated)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (includes plan for pituitary MRI and formal endocrine referral)

acceptable_variants.description: Performs contraindication checks explicitly before ordering and again before prescribing (to satisfy required safety checks) and documents them as separate steps if the benchmark expects explicit calls.
steps: ORDER_LAB (after contraindication_check-before-ordering is completed within ordering workflow or as a documented pre-order review)
PRESCRIBE (after contraindication_check-before-prescribing is completed within prescribing workflow or as a documented pre-prescription review)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 19-year-old college student/barista. Start with: “I get tired and weak really fast, especially later in the day.” Volunteer early: eyelids droop when tired; chewing/swallowing get hard if you eat a while; voice gets softer/nasal after talking; resting a few minutes helps a lot.

If asked about other symptoms, mention: legs feel heavy/tripping after short walks but better after breaks; trouble holding arms up (washing hair/reaching cabinets); occasional coughing/choking with drinks when very tired; short of breath when sick like chest muscles tire; symptoms since childhood; no numbness/tingling—strength just fades.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: worse with illness/stress/lack of sleep; heat worsens (hot showers/weather); flat facial expression; avoiding long meals; waking with headaches/unrefreshed. Only admit drooling if asked directly; act embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: you worried it’s anxiety, low blood sugar (you have diabetes), or being out of shape. Show cost anxiety: assume genetic tests/specialists aren’t covered; you avoid care unless “really sick” and may ration meds. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and open up more.

medications: Pyridostigmine 60 mg PO every 4 hours while awake
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO three times daily
Insulin glargine 18 units SC nightly
Insulin lispro SC with meals (carbohydrate ratio 1 unit per 10 g carbs) plus correction scale
Magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily
confounders: Hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia (can worsen neuromuscular transmission and mimic progression of weakness)
Poor glycemic control with intermittent dehydration/fatigue (can be misattributed to neuromuscular disease)
Recent viral upper respiratory infection with increased fatigue (can mimic myasthenic exacerbation)
Use of magnesium supplementation and exposure to macrolides/fluoroquinolones (drug-related worsening of neuromuscular weakness)

acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to acetylcholine receptor deficiency (AChR-related CMS)
Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to RAPSN-related postsynaptic defect
required_evidence: Chronic fatigable weakness with fluctuating symptoms (worse with exertion, improved with rest)
Ocular/bulbar involvement may be present (ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, nasal speech) without sensory loss
No autoimmune features typical of myasthenia gravis (e.g., lacks AChR/MuSK antibodies if tested; no thymoma history)
Electrophysiology compatible with neuromuscular junction disorder (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG) consistent with postsynaptic defect
History of symptom worsening with macrolides (azithromycin) consistent with impaired neuromuscular transmission
Onset in childhood/longstanding course and/or family history supporting congenital etiology

expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Potassium: Usually normal; abnormal potassium can mimic/worsen weakness and should be excluded
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - CO2 (bicarbonate: Usually normal; low CO2 may suggest respiratory alkalosis or metabolic acidosis; not diagnostic for CMS

3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO three times daily (titrate; max 80 mg/day; avoid if seizure risk)
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications that worsen neuromuscular transmission (macrolides including azithromycin, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, beta-blockers, certain anesthetics/neuromuscular blockers) unless essential and closely monitored
Energy conservation and activity pacing; schedule demanding tasks when strongest
Swallow/aspiration precautions if bulbar symptoms (speech/swallow evaluation; diet texture modification as needed)
Respiratory monitoring plan for intercurrent infections; prompt evaluation for dyspnea or declining forced vital capacity if available
Genetic counseling and referral to neuromuscular specialist for subtype confirmation and long-term management
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up in 2-4 weeks after medication initiation/titration (earlier within 48-72 hours if worsening weakness, dyspnea, or dysphagia)

must_include: Condition is a congenital (genetic) disorder of neuromuscular transmission affecting the postsynaptic side
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with exertion; not the same as autoimmune myasthenia gravis
Certain antibiotics/medications can worsen weakness (including macrolides such as azithromycin)
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness
How to take pyridostigmine (timing, expected benefit, common side effects such as abdominal cramps/diarrhea, and signs of overmedication such as increased secretions or worsening weakness)
If using amifampridine: gradual titration, seizure risk warning, and need to avoid exceeding maximum dose
Plan for reassessment and dose adjustment based on response and side effects
Red flags requiring urgent care: new/worsening shortness of breath, trouble swallowing, inability to clear secretions, rapidly progressive weakness

Medication list review to avoid interacting agents (e.g., magnesium-containing products, certain antibiotics)
Infection prevention and early treatment planning (vaccinations as appropriate; prompt evaluation for respiratory infections)
Activity pacing and sleep/heat management (overexertion and heat can worsen symptoms)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: CMT1 (demyelinating Charcot-Marie-Tooth) or CMT2 (axonal Charcot-Marie-Tooth) pending confirmatory testing
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (feet/legs > hands), often beginning in adolescence/early adulthood
Distal sensory loss (reduced vibration/proprioception) and decreased/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or high-steppage gait/foot drop
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (often autosomal dominant) OR onset pattern consistent with hereditary neuropathy
Electrodiagnostic testing consistent with peripheral neuropathy (nerve conduction studies showing demyelinating pattern with slowed conduction velocities or axonal pattern with reduced amplitudes)
Exclusion of common acquired causes of peripheral neuropathy (e.g., diabetes, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, toxin/medication exposure) by history and/or targeted testing as indicated

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for confirmation (nerve conduction studies/EMG) and consideration of genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, and gait training
Occupational therapy for hand weakness, adaptive devices, and energy conservation
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop; consider custom footwear/orthotics for pes cavus and pressure redistribution
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformity, recurrent ankle sprains, or ulcers; consider surgical options if severe
Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Foot care education (daily inspection, proper footwear) and skin protection to prevent ulcers
Avoid neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine, cisplatin, paclitaxel, high-dose pyridoxine, nitrofurantoin, metronidazole, amiodarone) and limit alcohol
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 4-8 weeks; PT/OT initiation within 1-2 weeks; reassess function, falls, and pain in 6-12 weeks

Symptoms typically progress gradually; severity varies widely even within families
Diagnosis is confirmed with nerve conduction studies/EMG and sometimes genetic testing
No curative therapy; management focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
PT/OT and bracing/orthotics can improve gait, reduce falls, and maintain mobility
When to consider orthopedic evaluation (worsening deformity, recurrent sprains, ulcers)
Discuss prognosis (slow progression) and individualized expectations

Red flags warranting urgent evaluation (rapid progression, new bowel/bladder issues, severe asymmetric weakness, acute sensory level)
Foot care and appropriate footwear to prevent skin breakdown

Avoid or review potentially neurotoxic medications and limit alcohol

Start with: “I can’t shake this cough, and I keep bringing up a lot of phlegm.” Volunteer early: cough most days for years but worse past 2 months; thick mucus especially mornings; more short of breath on stairs; tired/run down. Only admit if asked gently (embarrassed): urine leakage with hard coughing, foul-smelling mucus, avoiding social plans fearing you seem contagious.

required_evidence: Chronic productive cough and recurrent lower respiratory tract infections or exacerbations

Expected course includes exacerbations; goals are symptom control and reducing infections

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, exacerbation features, sputum volume/color, hemoptysis, dyspnea, fever, chest pain, functional status, and prior bronchiectasis history to determine acuity and need for urgent escalation.

Clarify past evaluation for causes (childhood infections, aspiration/GERD, immunodeficiency, ABPA/asthma, autoimmune disease, alpha-1, ciliary dyskinesia), prior imaging, prior sputum cultures (including Pseudomonas/NTM), and frequency/severity of exacerbations.

Obtain medication list including 'adenosine phosphate', OTC/supplements, prior antibiotic exposures, and detailed penicillin allergy history (reaction type, timing, severity, tolerance of cephalosporins).

Order sputum Gram stain/culture and sensitivity before antibiotics when feasible; consider AFB culture for NTM especially if considering long-term macrolide; consider baseline CBC/CMP and inflammatory markers if systemic symptoms.

Order evaluation for treatable causes when not previously done: serum immunoglobulins (IgG/IgA/IgM), total IgE and Aspergillus-specific testing if asthma/atopy, and other targeted tests based on history; ensure prior HRCT confirmation is documented or obtain if missing.
Review culture data (prior and current if available) and baseline labs to guide antibiotic choice and assess safety for proposed therapies.

Synthesize findings to confirm idiopathic bronchiectasis vs bronchiectasis with identifiable cause; classify current state (stable vs acute exacerbation) and document severity features and colonization risk.
Mandatory safety check before prescribing: verify penicillin allergy details and determine safe alternatives; consider whether cephalosporins are acceptable based on reaction history.

Mandatory safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., macrolides/fluoroquinolones with QT-prolonging agents, warfarin, statins) and interactions with 'adenosine phosphate' or other supplements.

Mandatory safety check before prescribing: assess contraindications such as significant QT prolongation/arrhythmia risk for macrolides, tendon/aortic risk for fluoroquinolones, renal/hepatic impairment for dosing, pregnancy considerations, and NTM status before long-term macrolide.

If an exacerbation is present, prescribe empiric antibiotics aligned with prior cultures/local guidance and allergy constraints, with plan to narrow/adjust based on sputum culture; avoid penicillins if true allergy; document duration and return precautions.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis (permanent airway widening, idiopathic meaning, expected course), explain treatment (airway clearance, culture-guided antibiotics, macrolide considerations incl NTM/QT/side effects), address concerns (penicillin allergy alternatives/testing, red flags, adenosine phosphate not standard), and lifestyle prevention (vaccines, hygiene, hydration/humidification, smoking avoidance, exercise).

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, exacerbation features, sputum volume/color, hemoptysis, dyspnea, fever, chest pain, functional status, and prior bronchiectasis history to determine acuity and need for urgent escalation.

Mandatory safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., macrolides/fluoroquinolones with QT-prolonging agents, warfarin, statins) and interactions with 'adenosine phosphate' or other supplements.

Mandatory safety check before prescribing: assess contraindications such as significant QT prolongation/arrhythmia risk for macrolides, tendon/aortic risk for fluoroquinolones, renal/hepatic impairment for dosing, pregnancy considerations, and NTM status before long-term macrolide.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi-like syndrome due to pathogenic point mutation (genetic obesity/hypotonia-hyperphagia syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Albright hereditary osteodystrophy / pseudohypoparathyroidism type 1A (GNAS-related, PWS-like phenotype)

required_evidence: Documented pathogenic point mutation consistent with a Prader-Willi-like phenotype (genetic report)
Clinical features consistent with PWS-like syndrome (e.g., hyperphagia/food-seeking, developmental delay/intellectual disability, hypotonia, obesity or rapid weight gain, short stature)
Evidence of hypogonadism or genital hypoplasia (hypoplastic labia minora) on exam and/or endocrine evaluation
Exclude or document evaluation for classic PWS mechanisms if not already done (DNA methylation testing for 15q11-q13 imprinting)

expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Often normal early; may be elevated if insulin resistance/diabetes has developed
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Often normal to mildly elevated; may be elevated if type 2 diabetes present
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Frequently elevated consistent with insulin resistance (especially with obesity)
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: May be elevated if dyslipidemia/metabolic syndrome present
expected_lab_results.HDL cholesterol: Often low in insulin resistance/metabolic syndrome
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be mildly elevated suggesting hepatic steatosis (NAFLD) in obesity/insulin resistance

medications: Somatotropin 0.24 mg/kg/week subcutaneous divided daily (or equivalent weight-based daily dosing per product) — continue if already prescribed and tolerated
non_pharmacological: Structured, externally controlled nutrition plan with caloric restriction appropriate for reduced energy expenditure; strict food security (locked/controlled access) to prevent hyperphagia-related harm
Regular physical activity plan and physiotherapy/strengthening as needed; aim for daily activity tailored to developmental level
Multidisciplinary care: endocrinology, nutrition, developmental/behavioral health, and (as indicated) gynecology for hypogonadism/genital hypoplasia evaluation
Metabolic surveillance and counseling for obesity-related complications (type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, NAFLD)
Screen for and address sleep-disordered breathing/OSA (especially important with growth hormone therapy)
Family/caregiver education on behavioral management strategies and safety (food-seeking, choking risk if relevant, routines)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 3 months for growth/weight trajectory, metabolic labs, and growth hormone safety; earlier (4-6 weeks) if symptoms of hyperglycemia, edema, headaches, or sleep worsening occur

must_include: This is a genetic condition causing a Prader-Willi-like pattern of symptoms (appetite/weight regulation and hormone differences)
The genital finding (hypoplastic labia minora) can be related to hypogonadism and warrants endocrine/gynecology evaluation
Genetic conditions are not caused by anything the family did; recurrence risk depends on the specific mutation and should be reviewed with genetics
Purpose of somatotropin: improve body composition (more lean mass, less fat), support growth, and potentially improve physical function
Key monitoring: glucose/A1c, lipids, liver enzymes, growth parameters, and sleep/OSA symptoms
When to seek urgent care: severe headaches/vision changes (concern for intracranial hypertension), new/worsening snoring or breathing pauses, marked swelling, or symptoms of high blood sugar
Discuss expected benefits vs risks of growth hormone and the need for ongoing monitoring
Acknowledge caregiver challenges with hyperphagia and behavioral rigidity; offer resources and multidisciplinary support
Confirm and document antibiotic allergy (amoxicillin-clavulanate rash) and what it means for future prescribing
Food security and consistent routines are medical necessities in PWS-like syndromes, not punishment
Diet should be structured and supervised; avoid unsupervised access to food
Encourage regular activity and sleep hygiene; emphasize screening for sleep apnea

Clinical context consistent with diffuse alveolar damage and absence of an identifiable cause after initial evaluation (e.g., no clear cardiogenic pulmonary edema, no clear infection/aspiration/toxin exposure)

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) pH: Often mildly alkalemic or near-normal early (e.g., ~7.45–7.50) due to respiratory alkalosis; may normalize or acidemia if fatigue/late failure
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) PaCO2: Low to normal early (e.g., ~28–35 mmHg) from tachypnea; may rise late if ventilatory failure develops
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) PaO2: Markedly decreased (often <60 mmHg; can be <50 mmHg) consistent with severe hypoxemia
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) HCO3-: Normal to mildly decreased (e.g., ~20–24 mEq/L) consistent with acute respiratory alkalosis compensation
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) SaO2: Reduced (often <90%; may be <85% depending on severity)
expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Often elevated (e.g., 12–20 x10^9/L) from stress/inflammation; can be normal
expected_lab_results.CBC Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be elevated if hemoconcentration/dehydration
expected_lab_results.CBC Platelets: Often normal early; can be decreased if severe systemic illness develops

non_pharmacological: Admit to ICU for close monitoring; early involvement of pulmonology/critical care

Evaluate and treat potential triggers while AIP is a diagnosis of exclusion (cultures/viral testing, imaging, echocardiography to assess for cardiogenic edema as indicated) without delaying supportive care

Review results to identify hypoxemia and to exclude common mimics (infection, heart failure, pulmonary embolism, aspiration, toxic inhalation, autoimmune disease) before concluding AIP; reassess need for ICU escalation.
Commit to acute interstitial pneumonia only after reasonable exclusion of alternative causes and recognition of ARDS-like presentation with rapid progression risk.

Explain diagnosis, uncertainty, prognosis, and likely ICU course; discuss supportive care priorities and steroid role/risks; address patient concerns and goals of care; provide lifestyle and prevention counseling for recovery phase.

Before any prescribing (e.g., empiric antibiotics if infection cannot be excluded or other meds), confirm allergy history (especially penicillin) and reaction type/severity to guide safe alternatives.
If prescribing is needed, screen for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions with home medications (non-critical but expected safety step).

Initiate appropriate therapies consistent with AIP/ARDS supportive care and local scope: oxygen escalation plan; consider steroids only after reasonable infection exclusion; ensure VTE prophylaxis and conservative fluids if in ICU setting; avoid contraindicated agents.
Arrange ICU/pulmonology follow-up as appropriate and post-acute follow-up planning (pulmonary rehab, vaccination timing, monitoring for fibrosis/functional decline) once stable.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting for deterioration, and documenting plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explains trigeminal neuralgia as irritation/misfiring of the trigeminal nerve causing brief, severe, shock-like unilateral facial pain in bursts, often triggered by light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, or cold air.

Agent explicitly addresses evaluation for secondary causes and states when imaging is recommended (e.g., atypical features such as sensory loss, bilateral symptoms, age <40, persistent pain between attacks, abnormal neuro exam, or concern for tumor/MS), and advises MRI brain with attention to trigeminal nerve when indicated.

Agent gathers core diagnostic features: distribution (V1/V2/V3), laterality, duration (seconds to 2 minutes), triggerability, refractory period, frequency, and inter-episode baseline pain; and screens for red flags (numbness, weakness, vision changes, headache, fever, rash/vesicles, dental pathology, trauma).
Agent selects first-line pharmacotherapy (carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine), starts low and titrates, and provides a clear plan for dose escalation and what to do if ineffective or not tolerated.

Agent communicates common side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, ataxia) and serious risks: rash/SJS/TEN (both; higher concern with carbamazepine), blood count abnormalities/agranulocytosis/aplastic anemia warning for carbamazepine, and hyponatremia risk (especially oxcarbazepine).

Agent provides monitoring guidance: check sodium (baseline and after initiation/titration); advises CBC monitoring strategy or symptom-triggered CBC (fever, sore throat, easy bruising/bleeding) for carbamazepine; and includes guidance to stop medication and seek urgent care for rash, fever, mouth sores, facial swelling, severe weakness/confusion, or signs of infection.

Agent completes required safety checks before prescribing: confirms allergy details (penicillin urticarial rash; codeine nausea/vomiting) and confirms no prior anticonvulsant reaction; performs contraindication screening; and performs drug interaction review including OTC/herbals.

Agent identifies high-risk interactions and counseling points: carbamazepine enzyme induction (reduced efficacy of hormonal contraception and many drugs), additive sedation with alcohol/benzodiazepines/opioids/antihistamines, and advises backup contraception or alternative method if relevant.

Agent provides safety counseling: avoid driving/operating machinery until medication effects are known; avoid alcohol and other sedatives; do not abruptly stop medication without guidance (unless severe reaction).

Agent provides lifestyle/trigger counseling (avoid known triggers, gentle oral hygiene, soft foods if needed) and clear urgent-care return precautions (new neuro deficits, persistent numbness/weakness, vision changes, severe headache, fever, rapidly worsening pain).

Agent mentions escalation options if medications fail or are not tolerated (neurology/neurosurgery referral for microvascular decompression, percutaneous rhizotomy procedures, stereotactic radiosurgery) without overpromising and with appropriate referral framing.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious administrative assistant. Speak in a tangential, worried way and jump between symptoms, work stress, and what you’ve tried.

Open with: crampy lower-belly pain that comes and goes, loose stools several times daily, strong urgency, and feeling more tired.

If asked, add: occasional blood mixed in stool, waking at night to poop, a few pounds of unintentional weight loss, feeling feverish sometimes, low appetite, intermittent nausea without vomiting.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: burning/sore anus after stools, recent painful mouth sores, achy knees/ankles, gritty/red eyes, greasy foods worsen symptoms, frequent ibuprofen use.

Resist/feel embarrassed about: a couple small accidents, mucus and very foul-smelling stool, avoiding sex due to soreness/urgency—only admit if the clinician asks directly and is nonjudgmental.

Express misconceptions: you think it’s food poisoning after eating out, or stress/“IBS,” and wonder about gluten/coffee/energy drinks.

Bring up cost/time concerns early: you want the fastest plan, fear surprise bills, may delay tests/meds if expensive.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, say you’re scared, and become more willing to share details.

medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03/0.15 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain
confounders: Recent long-haul flight (immobility within past week)
Combined oral contraceptive use (estrogen exposure)
Anxiety/panic symptoms can mimic cardiopulmonary disease
Recent NSAID use may mask fever/pleuritic pain

required_evidence: Elevated D-dimer in a patient with compatible symptoms (acute dyspnea/pleuritic chest pain/syncope/tachycardia) and no low-risk rule-out
ABG on room air showing hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis (low PaCO2) and widened A–a gradient
Cardiac strain pattern supported by elevated BNP and/or mild troponin elevation (possible in submassive PE)
Leukocytosis absent or mild (does not strongly support infection as sole cause)
Clinical need for confirmatory imaging (CT pulmonary angiography or V/Q scan) and risk stratification

expected_lab_results.D-dimer: Elevated (typically >500 ng/mL FEU; may be markedly elevated depending on clot burden and age-adjusted threshold).
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Normal to mildly elevated (can be elevated due to RV strain in intermediate-risk PE).
expected_lab_results.BNP: Normal to elevated (elevation suggests RV strain and higher-risk PE).
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaO2: Low (hypoxemia; e.g., <80 mmHg, often <70 depending on severity).
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - HCO3-: Normal to mildly low (acute respiratory alkalosis; e.g., 20–24 mEq/L).
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal or mildly elevated (often <12–13 x10^9/L; marked leukocytosis would suggest infection/other causes).

medications: Oxygen to maintain SpO2 92-96% (titrate via nasal cannula or mask as needed)
Unfractionated heparin IV bolus 80 units/kg then infusion 18 units/kg/hr (titrate to aPTT/anti-Xa per protocol) OR enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h if hemodynamically stable and no contraindications
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/IV q6h PRN pain/fever (avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk or pending anticoagulation decisions)
non_pharmacological: Immediate risk stratification: assess hemodynamics, oxygen requirement, signs of RV strain; calculate PESI/sPESI
Order confirmatory imaging: CT pulmonary angiography (first-line) or V/Q scan if CTPA contraindicated; obtain ECG and bedside echocardiography if concern for RV dysfunction
Continuous pulse oximetry and cardiac monitoring; establish IV access
If massive PE with hypotension/shock: activate emergency pathway for systemic thrombolysis (e.g., alteplase) or catheter-directed therapy in consultation with ICU/PE response team
Evaluate bleeding risk and contraindications to anticoagulation; consider IVC filter only if acute PE and absolute contraindication to anticoagulation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour after initiating oxygen/anticoagulation; definitive imaging and disposition (ICU vs step-down vs ward) within 4-6 hours; anticoagulation plan and outpatient follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge (or sooner if high risk).

Need for urgent confirmatory imaging (CT pulmonary angiography or V/Q scan) and monitoring
Why labs (D-dimer, troponin, BNP, ABG) support suspicion but do not alone confirm PE
Start anticoagulation to prevent clot growth and new clots while diagnosis is confirmed/treated
Bleeding risks and warning signs (black stools, vomiting blood, severe headache, easy bruising) and when to seek immediate help
Possible escalation to thrombolysis/catheter procedure if unstable or evidence of severe right-heart strain

Review allergies (penicillin hives; shellfish lip swelling) and clarify that shellfish allergy does not automatically preclude iodinated contrast, but prior contrast reactions and asthma history should be checked
Discuss pregnancy status if applicable and implications for imaging/anticoagulation choice
Confirm no history of major bleeding, recent surgery, hemorrhagic stroke, or uncontrolled severe hypertension before anticoagulation/thrombolysis
Clot risk reduction: early mobilization, hydration, avoiding prolonged immobility; travel precautions
Importance of adherence to anticoagulant regimen and follow-up for duration planning (provoked vs unprovoked PE)
Avoid NSAIDs and high-risk activities while anticoagulated; inform all clinicians/dentists about anticoagulant use

Required before prescribing; confirm and document medication allergies and reactions (e.g., penicillin causes hives) to guide empiric antibiotic selection if infection is suspected.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing; assess interactions with planned therapies (e.g., macrolides/fluoroquinolones, anticoagulants, sedatives) and home meds including pirfenidone.

Initiate supportive and empiric management consistent with severity: oxygen escalation (including high-flow), consider ICU/ventilation planning; if infection cannot be excluded, start empiric antibiotics adjusted for penicillin allergy; consider systemic corticosteroids after reasonable exclusion of infection/other causes and document uncertainty of benefit.

Communicate diagnosis, seriousness/ARDS-like course, exclusion nature; explain treatment priorities (oxygen/ventilation, possible steroids, pirfenidone not acute rescue); address concerns including goals of care and allergy implications; provide lifestyle/vaccine and return precautions.
Arrange appropriate disposition and follow-up: ICU/pulmonology consult if severe; if improving/discharged, close follow-up, oxygen/rehab plan, and monitoring instructions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting decisions, and ensuring safety checks and milestones completed.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, duration, severity, functional impact, and immediate red flags (chest pain, syncope, dyspnea at rest, melena, heavy bleeding) to assess stability and urgency.

Targeted history for microcytic anemia/iron deficiency: menstrual history (volume, frequency), GI symptoms (melena/hematochezia, abdominal pain), diet, pica, pregnancy possibility, prior anemia, family history, and medication use (NSAIDs, anticoagulants, PPIs).

Order initial anemia workup and iron studies to confirm iron deficiency and assess severity (e.g., CBC with indices/retic, ferritin, iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation; consider pregnancy test if relevant; consider stool occult blood based on context).
Review labs to determine anemia type and likelihood of iron deficiency (e.g., low Hgb with low MCV and low ferritin/TSAT).
Synthesize findings: anemia with small red blood cells most consistent with iron deficiency; document differential and need to evaluate bleeding/absorption causes.
Explain diagnosis and the need to find/treat the underlying cause, not just replace iron; discuss likely sources of chronic blood loss (gynecologic/GI) and inadequate intake/absorption.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm and document allergy history, including penicillin allergy details (reaction, timing, severity), and note current plan does not require penicillin.

Non-critical safety step before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interactions that impair iron absorption or increase bleeding risk (calcium, antacids, PPIs, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/quinolones, NSAIDs/anticoagulants).
Start oral iron therapy with evidence-based dosing (every-other-day elemental iron) and provide instructions to optimize absorption and mitigate side effects.
Explain treatment: how to take iron (away from calcium/antacids; consider vitamin C), expected timeline for improvement and hemoglobin rise, and common GI side effects with management strategies.

Lifestyle and adherence counseling: iron-rich diet, spacing from inhibitors, importance of adherence, and continuing iron after normalization to replete stores; review red flags requiring urgent care.
Arrange follow-up labs and evaluation for bleeding source (recheck CBC/retic in ~2-4 weeks; ferritin later to confirm repletion; plan gynecologic/GI evaluation as indicated).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Act evasive about embarrassing topics and money. Start by saying: “She’s been losing skills and having weird hand movements, and now she has spells that look like seizures.” Volunteer only: she used to say a few words but mostly stopped over the last year; repetitive hand-wringing/rubbing (worse when upset/excited); staring spells with lip smacking; clumsy wide-based walking with more falls; irritable/tearful. If asked directly, add: trouble feeding herself; hands mostly to mouth/rubbing; poor sleep with laughing/screaming; breath-holding/rapid breathing bursts; bruxism/chewing; constipation; variable social interest; startles/overwhelmed by noise. Do not mention hidden/resistant symptoms unless the clinician is specific and nonjudgmental. If asked about causes, offer misconceptions: autism/ADHD, vaccines/exposure, anxiety/panic, cerebral palsy. Express cost fears: genetic tests/specialists “extra,” worry labels hurt insurance, therapies not worth it unless private daily, seizure meds cause dependency—prefer supplements. If clinician shows empathy, briefly soften, nod/tearful, then shut down again.

medications: levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily
sertraline 50 mg once daily
omeprazole 20 mg once daily
polyethylene glycol 17 g once daily
baclofen 10 mg three times daily
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU once daily
ferrous sulfate 325 mg every other day
confounders: cerebral palsy diagnosis in childhood complicating recognition of regression and stereotypies
episodes of hyperventilation/breath-holding causing respiratory alkalosis that may mimic panic attacks or asthma
SSRI use (sertraline) and low magnesium contributing to QTc prolongation risk
low BMI and micronutrient deficiencies from feeding difficulties masking as primary GI disease
antiepileptic therapy effects on mood/behavior complicating psychiatric assessment

acceptable_alternatives: CDKL5-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (Rett-like phenotype)
required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental regression after a period of early development (often 6–18 months), with loss of acquired purposeful hand skills and/or spoken language
Stereotyped hand movements (e.g., hand-wringing, washing, clapping, mouthing) and gait abnormalities
Supportive features common in atypical Rett: seizures, breathing dysregulation, sleep disturbance, scoliosis, growth failure, autonomic/GI dysfunction
Exclusion of an acquired brain injury or metabolic/toxic cause explaining regression
Genetic confirmation when available: pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant in MECP2 (or, if negative with Rett-like phenotype, consideration of CDKL5/FOXG1 testing)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be low if comorbid iron deficiency from poor intake/feeding difficulties
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal; may be low (microcytosis) if iron deficiency present
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal; may be low consistent with iron deficiency (interpret with inflammation status)
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Normal; may be low in iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.TIBC: Normal; may be high in iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.Transferrin saturation: Normal; may be low (<20%) in iron deficiency

medications: Iron (elemental) 3 mg/kg/day PO once daily (if ferritin low and/or transferrin saturation low consistent with iron deficiency)
Vitamin D3 600–1000 IU PO daily (if dietary intake/sun exposure inadequate; target per age and local guidance)
Polyethylene glycol 3350 0.4 g/kg/day PO daily (if constipation present)
non_pharmacological: Refer to pediatric neurology/genetics for confirmation and Rett-spectrum management; obtain/confirm MECP2 testing and consider CDKL5/FOXG1 if MECP2 negative with Rett-like phenotype
Multidisciplinary care: PT/OT/speech/feeding therapy; augmentative and alternative communication assessment
Seizure screening and management plan (history, EEG if clinically indicated); rescue medication plan if seizures occur
Nutrition/feeding evaluation (risk of aspiration, growth monitoring); consider swallow study if coughing/choking, recurrent pneumonias, or prolonged feeds
Screen and manage scoliosis/hip subluxation (orthopedics), and monitor mobility and contractures
Sleep and breathing dysregulation assessment; consider pulmonology/sleep medicine if significant apnea/hyperventilation
Caregiver support and anticipatory guidance; connect to Rett resources and community services
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4–8 weeks for symptom review and lab response if iron started; otherwise coordinate specialty follow-up within 1–3 months and ongoing multidisciplinary visits every 3–6 months

must_include: Atypical Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by regression and specific motor/hand stereotypies; symptoms are due to brain development differences, not parenting
Discuss the role of genetics (often MECP2) and that genetic testing can confirm and guide counseling
Set expectations: symptoms can be managed and function optimized, but it is a lifelong condition requiring multidisciplinary care
Treatment is supportive and symptom-directed (therapy services, seizure management if present, nutrition/feeding, orthopedic monitoring)
If iron deficiency is found, explain iron dosing, how to take it (with vitamin C-containing beverage if tolerated; avoid taking with calcium/antacids), and expected timeline for improvement
Clear follow-up plan and which red flags warrant urgent evaluation (new seizures, breathing pauses/cyanosis, aspiration signs, dehydration)
Acknowledge caregiver stress and provide resources (Rett foundations, early intervention, school supports, respite care)
Discuss prognosis variability in atypical Rett and the importance of ongoing reassessment
Offer genetic counseling for recurrence risk and family planning
Safe feeding positioning and aspiration precautions if feeding difficulties present
Bowel regimen and hydration/fiber guidance if constipation present
Skin care and avoidance strategies for known latex allergy; ensure latex-free medical environments

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: developmental regression pattern, hand stereotypies, seizures/breathing irregularities, feeding/aspiration, constipation, sleep, growth, prior testing/therapies, meds/supplements, and caregiver goals.

Order targeted evaluation to support symptom-directed management and common comorbidities (e.g., CBC, ferritin/iron studies if fatigue/pica/poor intake; CMP; vitamin D if bone risk; consider TSH if growth/constipation; consider swallow evaluation referral outside tools).
Interpret results to guide treatment (e.g., confirm iron deficiency before iron therapy; assess for anemia/electrolyte issues).

State working diagnosis of atypical Rett syndrome (or high suspicion) based on regression and characteristic features; recommend confirmatory genetic testing (often MECP2; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 depending on phenotype) and multidisciplinary care plan.

Explain diagnosis, genetics, prognosis variability, and supportive/symptom-directed treatment approach; provide red flags and caregiver resources; include lifestyle/feeding/constipation/latex-avoidance counseling.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; specifically confirm latex allergy and medication allergies to ensure latex-free environment and avoid allergenic excipients if relevant.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; assess interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., iron with calcium/antacids; antiepileptics if present).

If indicated by results, prescribe symptom-directed therapy (e.g., oral iron for confirmed iron deficiency) with correct dosing and administration counseling; avoid prescribing if contraindications present.
Arrange follow-up to review genetics/testing plans, monitor symptoms (seizures, breathing, feeding, growth), reassess labs (iron response), and coordinate multidisciplinary services.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing written plan/resources, and ensuring urgent return precautions.

nl_assertions: Agent obtains a focused neurodevelopmental history including timing of regression, loss of purposeful hand use, hand stereotypies, gait abnormalities, communication changes, and triggers/worsening factors.

Agent screens for common Rett comorbidities: seizures (type/frequency), breathing dysrhythmias (hyperventilation/apneas/cyanosis), feeding/swallowing difficulties and aspiration signs, constipation, sleep disturbance, scoliosis/orthopedic issues, and growth/nutrition concerns.
Agent explicitly states that atypical Rett syndrome is neurodevelopmental (brain development differences), not caused by parenting, and uses clear, non-stigmatizing language.

Agent discusses genetics (often MECP2) and the purpose/limitations of genetic testing, including confirmation and counseling implications; offers genetic counseling for recurrence risk/family planning.

Agent sets expectations appropriately: lifelong condition with supportive, symptom-directed management to optimize function and quality of life; emphasizes multidisciplinary care (neurology, genetics, PT/OT/SLP, nutrition, orthopedics, pulmonology/ENT as needed).

If iron deficiency is identified/treated, agent provides correct oral iron guidance: element iron dosing appropriate for pediatrics, administration with vitamin C-containing beverage if tolerated, separation from calcium/antacids, common side effects (GI upset/constipation/dark stools), and timeline for improvement with recheck plan.

Agent provides a clear urgent-evaluation list including: new or worsening seizures, breathing pauses/cyanosis, aspiration signs (coughing/choking with feeds, recurrent pneumonia), dehydration/poor intake, and significant lethargy.
Agent acknowledges caregiver stress, assesses support needs, and offers resources (Rett foundations, early intervention, school supports/IEP, respite care, social work).

Agent provides lifestyle counseling tailored to symptoms: safe feeding positioning and aspiration precautions if feeding issues; constipation prevention/management with hydration and fiber guidance; skin care and strict latex avoidance strategies with reminder to request latex-free medical environments.
Before any prescribing, agent performs critical safety checks: allergy check and contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical error for benchmark scoring.
Agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when feasible; omission is non-critical but should reduce quality score.
Agent avoids overpromising outcomes, uses shared decision-making, and verifies caregiver understanding via teach-back or equivalent.

minimal_paths.description: High suspicion/diagnosis explanation and supportive plan without initiating medications (e.g., no iron deficiency or no prescribing needed).
steps: ASK: focused Rett features + comorbidities + caregiver goals
DIAGNOSE: atypical Rett syndrome/high suspicion; recommend genetic testing and multidisciplinary referrals
EDUCATE: diagnosis/genetics/prognosis variability; supportive care; red flags; resources; lifestyle counseling incl latex avoidance
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: genetics/testing review and symptom monitoring

minimal_paths.description: Diagnosis discussion plus lab-confirmed iron deficiency treated with oral iron, including required safety checks before prescribing.
steps: ASK: Rett features + comorbidities + nutrition/diet + meds/allergies
ORDER_LAB: CBC, ferritin, iron/TIBC (±CRP if inflammatory concern)
GET_RESULTS: confirm iron deficiency (low ferritin/iron indices ± anemia)
DIAGNOSE: atypical Rett syndrome/high suspicion; discuss genetic testing
EDUCATE: diagnosis/genetics/lifelong supportive care; iron education framework; red flags; resources; lifestyle counseling incl constipation and latex avoidance
CHECK_ALLERGY: verify medication allergies and latex allergy status
CHECK_INTERACTION: check calcium/antacid use, other supplements/meds
PRESCRIBE: oral elemental iron with appropriate pediatric dosing and administration instructions; plan for side effects and recheck
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: 4-8 week lab/clinical response check; multidisciplinary coordination

acceptable_variants.description: Contraindication identified (e.g., inability to tolerate oral iron, concern for iron overload history, or unclear diagnosis): agent defers prescribing, pursues further evaluation/referral while still completing education and safety counseling.
Agent performs safety checks earlier (allergy/interaction/contraindication) before ordering or before results; still must occur before any PRESCRIBE action.

Agent includes additional symptom-directed non-prescription interventions (e.g., constipation regimen counseling, feeding therapy referrals) within EDUCATE and follow-up planning, without changing required safety sequence for any medication.
